\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.13} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Sacrifice performed without regard to proper procedure, without distribution of food, without mantras, without gifts, and devoid of faith is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.14} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Worship of the devas, brahmins, teachers, and the wise, purity, straightforwardness, celibacy, and nonviolence—these are said to be bodily austerity.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.15} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Speech that causes no agitation, is truthful, pleasant, and beneficial, and also the regular recitation and study of the Vedas—this is called austerity of speech.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.16} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Serenity of mind, gentleness, silence, and self-restraint: this is called mental austerity.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.17} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The threefold austerity performed by men with supreme faith, by those who are steadfast and seek no reward, is called sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.18} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Whatever austerity is performed here for the sake of honor, respect, and worship, and indeed with hypocrisy—that is said to be rajasic, unstable, and impermanent.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.19} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Austerity that is performed with foolish obstinacy, by self-torment, or for the purpose of harming another, is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.20} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
A gift given as a duty, without expectation of return, at the proper place and time, and to a worthy recipient, is regarded as sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.21} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But whatever is given with expectation of return, or with a desire for reward, and given grudgingly, that gift is regarded as rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.22} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
A gift given at the wrong place and time, and to unworthy recipients, given without respect and with contempt, is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.23} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Om, Tat, Sat—this has been declared as the threefold designation of Brahman. By that the brāhmaṇas, the Vedas, and sacrifices were ordained in ancient times.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.24} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Therefore, the acts of sacrifice, gift-giving, and austerity, as prescribed by the injunctions, are always undertaken by the expounders of Brahman, uttering Om.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.25} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Saying “tat,” without aiming at the fruit, acts of sacrifice, austerity, and various acts of giving are performed by those who desire liberation.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.26} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The word sat is used in the sense of being and in the sense of goodness; and, O Partha, the word sat is likewise applied to a praiseworthy action.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.27} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Steadfastness in sacrifice, austerity, and charity is also called satt; and action performed for that purpose is likewise called satt.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{17.28} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Whatever is offered, given, or practiced in austerity without faith, O Pārtha, is called asat; it is neither here nor after death.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\clearpage
# Chapter 18 - Moksha-Sannyasa Yoga

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.1} \textit{(Arjuna)}\par
Mighty-armed one, I wish to know the true nature of renunciation and of tyāga separately, O Hṛṣīkeśa, slayer of Keśin.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.2} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The wise understand renunciation to be the giving up of desire-motivated actions. The discerning declare that tyaga is the relinquishment of the fruits of all actions.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.3} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Some sages say that action should be abandoned as defective; others say that the acts of sacrifice, charity, and austerity should not be abandoned.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.4} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Listen from me now to the certainty regarding that renunciation, O best of the Bharatas. For renunciation, O tiger among men, is said to be of three kinds.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.5} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Sacrifice, charity, and austerity should not be abandoned; they must indeed be performed. For the wise, sacrifice, charity, and austerity are purifying acts.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.6} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But even these actions should be performed, giving up attachment and the fruits of action. This is, O Partha, my firm and highest opinion.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.7} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But renunciation of prescribed action is not proper; abandoning it out of delusion is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.8} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
If one abandons action because it is painful, from fear of bodily strain, he performs rajasic renunciation and does not obtain the fruit of renunciation.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.9} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
O Arjuna, that prescribed action which is performed simply because it ought to be done, abandoning attachment and also the fruit, is regarded as sattvic renunciation.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.10} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
He does not hate unwholesome action, nor is he attached to wholesome action. The renunciant, filled with sattva, is wise, and his doubts are cut away.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.11} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
For the embodied one, it is indeed not possible to renounce actions entirely. But one who renounces the fruits of action is called a renouncer.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.12} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
For those who have not renounced, after death there are three kinds of the fruit of action: undesirable, desirable, and mixed; but for those who have renounced, there is none at all.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.13} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
O mighty-armed one, learn from Me these five causes, as taught in Sāṅkhya for the accomplishment of all actions.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.14} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The basis, the agent, and the various instruments, and the many and different kinds of action, and also fate as the fifth.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.15} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Whatever action a person undertakes with the body, speech, and mind, whether right or wrong, these five are its causes.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.16} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Therefore, in this matter, one who, because of undeveloped understanding, sees the Self alone as the sole doer—he, of confused mind, does not see.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.17} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
One whose sense of ego is absent, whose intelligence is not tainted, even if he kills all these worlds, he does not kill, and is not bound.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.18} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Knowledge, the knowable, and the knower—these are the threefold impulse to action. The doer, the act, and the instrument are the threefold basis of karma.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.19} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Knowledge, action, and the doer are likewise said, in the classification of the gunas, to be threefold according to the distinctions among the gunas. Listen also to them as they are.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.20} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Know that knowledge to be sattvic by which one sees the one imperishable reality in all beings, the undivided in the divided.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.21} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But that knowledge by which one sees separate, diverse existences in all beings, know that knowledge to be rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.22} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But that which, clinging to a single task as if it were the whole, is groundless, and which is trivial and without regard for true purpose—that is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.23} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Action that is obligatory, done without attachment and without desire or aversion to results, is said to be sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.24} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But whatever action is performed by one who desires pleasure, or again by egotism, and is done with great effort, that is called rajas.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.25} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Action that is undertaken out of delusion, without regard for consequence, loss, injury, or one’s own capacity, is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.26} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Free from attachment, free from egoism, endowed with firmness and enthusiasm, and unchanged in success and failure, such a doer is said to be sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.27} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Passionate, seeking the fruits of action, greedy, violent, and impure, elated and sorrowful, such a doer is said to be rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.28} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Undisciplined, crude, obstinate, deceitful, dishonest, lazy, despondent, and procrastinating: such a doer is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.29} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Hear now, O Dhananjaya, in full and distinctly, the threefold distinction of buddhi and dhriti according to the gunas.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.30} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
And, O Partha, that intellect is sattvic which understands engagement and renunciation, what should be done and what should not be done, fear and fearlessness, bondage and liberation.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.31} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
That intellect by which one does not truly know dharma and adharma, and what should be done and what should not be done—that intellect, O Partha, is rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.32} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
That intellect, O Partha, which is veiled by tamas, mistakes adharma for dharma and regards all things in contrary ways, is tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.33} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The steadfastness by which one sustains the activities of the mind, the life-force, and the senses through unwavering yoga—that steadfastness, O Partha, is sattvic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.34} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
But that firmness, O Arjuna, by which one upholds dharma, kama, and artha with attachment to results, O Partha, is rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.35} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The steadfastness by which the dull-minded does not abandon sleep, fear, sorrow, dejection, and also delusion, O Partha, that steadfastness is tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.36} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Now hear from me, O best of the Bharatas, the threefold happiness: that in which one takes delight through practice and comes to the end of suffering.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.37} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
That pleasure which at first is like poison but in the end is like nectar is said to be sattvic, born of the clarity of self-understanding.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.38} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
That pleasure which arises from the contact of the senses with their objects, appearing like nectar at first, but like poison in the end, is remembered as rajasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.39} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
That happiness which, at first and in the end, deludes the self, and which arises from sleep, laziness, and negligence—that is said to be tamasic.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.40} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
There is nothing on earth, or in heaven among the devas, that is free from these three gunas born of prakriti.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.41} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
O scorcher of enemies, the duties of the brāhmaṇas, kshatriyas, vaishyas, and śūdras are divided according to the guṇas born of their own nature.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.42} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Tranquility, self-restraint, austerity, purity, forbearance, and straightforwardness, knowledge, realization, faith, and the duties proper to a Brahmin, born of one’s own nature.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.43} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Valor, splendor, steadfastness, dexterity, and not fleeing in battle, generosity, and lordly bearing—these are the natural duties of a kshatriya.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.44} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Agriculture, cattle-rearing, and trade are the natural duties of the vaisya; and service is the natural duty even of the shudra.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.45} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
A person, devoted to his own duty, attains perfection. Hear now how one devoted to his own duty attains success.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.46} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
From whom the movement of all beings has arisen, and by whom all this is pervaded, by worshiping Him through one’s own action, a human being attains perfection.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.47} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Better is one’s own dharma, though imperfect, than another’s dharma well performed. By doing the karma ordained by one’s own nature, one incurs no sin.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.48} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
O son of Kunti, one should not abandon the action born of one’s nature, even if it has faults. For all undertakings are covered by defect, as fire is by smoke.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.49} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
One whose understanding is unattached everywhere, who has subdued the self, and who is free from longing, attains the highest perfection of non-action through renunciation.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.50} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Listen from me, O Kaunteya, how one who has attained siddhi likewise attains Brahman, in brief—the supreme consummation of knowledge.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.51} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Endowed with purified intellect and restraining the self with steadfastness, having cast aside sense objects such as sound, and having given up attachment and aversion,
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.52} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
One who dwells in solitude, eats lightly, and has controlled speech, body, and mind; ever devoted to meditation and yoga, and established in detachment.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.53} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Free from ego, strength, arrogance, lust, anger, and possessiveness, and without a sense of “mine,” one becomes fit for the state of Brahman.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.54} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
One who has become Brahman and is of serene self neither grieves nor desires; being equal to all beings, he attains supreme devotion to Me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.55} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
By devotion he comes to know Me, as I am in truth; and then, knowing Me in truth, he enters into Me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.56} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Always performing all actions, taking refuge in Me, he attains by My grace the eternal, imperishable state.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.57} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
With your mind surrendering all actions to Me, intent on Me, take refuge in buddhi-yoga and always keep your mind fixed on Me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.58} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
With your mind set on Me, by My grace you will cross over all obstacles. But if, because of egoism, you do not listen, you will perish.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.59} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
If, relying on ego, you think, “I will not fight,” this resolve of yours is vain; prakriti will compel you.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.60} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
O son of Kunti, bound by your own karma, born of your own nature, you do not wish to do that which, through delusion, you will nevertheless do, even against your will.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.61} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
The Lord dwells in the heart-region of all beings, Arjuna, causing all beings to revolve, as if mounted on a machine, by means of maya.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.62} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Take refuge in Him alone with all your being, O Bharata; by His grace you will attain supreme peace and the eternal abode.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.63} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Thus I have explained to you this knowledge, more secret than all secrets. Reflect on this fully, and then do as you wish.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.64} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Listen again to My supreme, most secret words. You are dear to Me, and so I shall speak to you what is for your good.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.65} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Fix your mind on Me, be devoted to Me, sacrifice to Me, and bow down to Me. You will surely come to Me; I promise you truly, for you are dear to Me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.66} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Abandon all dharmas and take refuge in Me alone. I will मुक्त you from all sins; do not grieve.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.67} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
This is never to be spoken by you to one who is not disciplined, to one who is not devoted, nor to one who does not listen in service, nor to one who finds fault with me.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.68} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Whoever shall declare this supreme secret among My devotees, having shown supreme devotion to Me, will surely come to Me without doubt.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.69} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
And among all humans, there is none who does more for Me; nor will there ever be on earth anyone dearer to Me than he.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.70} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
And whoever studies this righteous dialogue of ours, by the yajna of knowledge, to him I shall be worshiped in this way—such is my view.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.71} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
Any man who hears this, with faith and without jealousy, he too, being freed, will attain the auspicious worlds of those who perform meritorious deeds.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.72} \textit{(Shri Bhagavan Krishna)}\par
O son of Pṛthā, have you heard this with an attentive mind? O Dhananjaya, has your delusion born of ignorance been dispelled?
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.73} \textit{(Arjuna)}\par
My delusion is destroyed, and my memory has returned by your grace, O Acyuta. I stand firm, free from doubt; I will do your word.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.74} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
Thus I heard this wondrous, thrilling dialogue between Vāsudeva and the great-souled Pārtha.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.75} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
By the grace of Vyāsa I have heard this supreme, most secret yoga directly from Kṛṣṇa, the lord of yoga, who spoke it himself.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.76} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
O King, remembering again and again this wondrous conversation of Keshava and Arjuna, I rejoice again and again.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.77} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
Remembering that wondrous form of Hari again and again, great is my amazement, O King, and I rejoice again and again.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\begingroup
\setlength{\parindent}{0pt}
\textbf{18.78} \textit{(Sanjaya)}\par
Wherever Krishna, the lord of yoga, is, and wherever Partha, the archer, is, there are prosperity, victory, splendor, and steadfast policy; that is my conviction.
\par\endgroup
\vspace{0.42em}

\clearpage
# Glossary

## Concepts

\begin{description}
\item[Atman] Self or inner principle; kept distinct from ordinary ego or personality. \textit{Forms: Atman | Ātman}
\item[bhakti] Devotion or devoted participation, especially toward the divine. \textit{Forms: bhakti | bhakti-yoga}
\item[Brahman] The ultimate reality or absolute principle. \textit{Forms: Brahman | Brahma-nirvana | Brahman-nirvana | Brahma-yoga | Brahma-sūtra | Brahman-realization}
\item[buddhi] Intellect, discernment, or decisive understanding. \textit{Forms: buddhi | buddhi-yoga}
\item[dharma] Order, duty, law, or right conduct; the exact sense depends on context. \textit{Forms: dharma | dharma-field}
\item[guna] A quality or constituent tendency of prakriti: sattva, rajas, and tamas. \textit{Forms: guna | gunas | guṇa | guṇas}
\item[karma] Action, deed, or the consequences bound to action. \textit{Forms: karma | karma-yoga}
\item[kshetra] Field, especially the body or field of experience in Chapter 13. \textit{Forms: kshetra | kṣetra}
\item[kshetrajna] Knower of the field; the conscious principle related to kshetra. \textit{Forms: kshetrajna | kṣetrajña}
\item[maya] Power of appearance, concealment, or manifestation; context-dependent. \textit{Forms: maya | Maya | māyā | Māyā | yoga-maya}
\item[nirvana] Extinction, peace, or release; here often linked with Brahman. \textit{Forms: nirvana | nirvāṇa | Brahma-nirvana | Brahman-nirvana}
\item[prakriti] Nature, material process, or the field of change and manifestation. \textit{Forms: prakriti | Prakriti | prakṛti | Prakṛti}
\item[purusha] Person, spirit, or conscious principle; meaning varies by passage. \textit{Forms: purusha | Purusha | puruṣa | Puruṣa | Purushottama}
\item[rajas] The guna associated with activity, passion, impulse, and restlessness. \textit{Forms: rajas | rājasika | Rajas}
\item[samsara] The ongoing cycle of worldly becoming, birth, death, and attachment. \textit{Forms: saṃsāra | samsara}
\item[Sankhya] A path or framework of discriminative knowledge; here not always a formal school label. \textit{Forms: Sankhya | Sāṅkhya | Sāṅkhyas | Sāṅkhya-yoga}
\item[sannyasa] Renunciation or giving up, especially of possessive attachment to action. \textit{Forms: sannyasa | sannyāsa}
\item[sattva] The guna associated with clarity, balance, and illumination. \textit{Forms: sattva | sāttvika | Sattva}
\item[tamas] The guna associated with inertia, dullness, confusion, and obstruction. \textit{Forms: tamas | tāmasika | Tamas}
\item[tyaga] Abandonment, renunciation, or relinquishing attachment to the fruits of action. \textit{Forms: tyāga | tyaga}
\item[yoga] Discipline, practice, integration, or path toward steadiness and liberation. Literal meaning is "yoking", "uniting", "joining" (from the root yuj: "to yoke", "to unite"). \textit{Forms: yoga | yogī | yogi | karma-yoga | buddhi-yoga | Sāṅkhya-yoga | bhakti-yoga | practice-yoga | yoga-maya}
\end{description}

## Social and ritual terms

\begin{description}
\item[acharya] Teacher or preceptor. \textit{Forms: ācārya | acharya}
\item[Brahmin] A member of the priestly or learned class; Sanskrit brāhmaṇa. \textit{Forms: Brahmin | brahmin | brāhmaṇa | brāhmaṇas}
\item[kshatriya] Member of the warrior or ruling class. \textit{Forms: kshatriya | kṣatriya}
\item[Om] Sacred syllable used as a concise sign of the absolute or divine. \textit{Forms: Om | AUM}
\item[shudra] Member of the service class in the traditional varna scheme. \textit{Forms: śūdra | śūdras | shudra | shudras}
\item[vaishya] Member of the class associated with trade, agriculture, and herding. \textit{Forms: vaiśya | vaiśyas | vaishya | vaishyas}
\item[Vedas] Ancient sacred texts of the Vedic tradition. \textit{Forms: Veda | Vedas | Ṛg | Sāma | Bṛhat-sāma | Gāyatrī}
\item[yajna] Sacrifice, ritual offering, or consecrated action. \textit{Forms: yajna | yajña | adhiyajña}
\end{description}

## Names and epithets

\begin{description}
\item[Acyuta] Epithet of Krishna, often glossed as the unfailing one. \textit{Forms: Acyuta}
\item[Arjuna] Arjuna, the warrior addressed by Krishna. \textit{Forms: Arjuna}
\item[Bharata] A dynastic or ancestral name, often used as an address. \textit{Forms: Bharata | Bhārata | Bharatas | Bhāratas}
\item[Dhananjaya] Epithet of Arjuna, often understood as winner of wealth. \textit{Forms: Dhananjaya | Dhanañjaya}
\item[Govinda] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Govinda}
\item[Gudakesha] Epithet of Arjuna, commonly linked with mastery over sleep. \textit{Forms: Guḍākeśa | Gudakesha}
\item[Hrishikesha] Epithet of Krishna, often understood as lord of the senses. \textit{Forms: Hṛṣīkeśa | Hrishikesha}
\item[Janardana] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Janardana | Janārdana}
\item[Kaunteya] Epithet of Arjuna: son of Kunti. \textit{Forms: Kaunteya | Kunti}
\item[Keshava] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Keshava | Keśava}
\item[Krishna] Krishna, speaker of the teaching to Arjuna. \textit{Forms: Krishna | Kṛṣṇa}
\item[Madhava] Epithet of Krishna. \textit{Forms: Madhava | Mādhava}
\item[Madhusudana] Epithet of Krishna: slayer of Madhu. \textit{Forms: Madhusudana | Madhusūdana}
\item[Pandava] One of the sons or descendants of Pandu; often refers to Arjuna and his brothers. \textit{Forms: Pāṇḍava | Pāṇḍavas | Pandava | Pandavas | Pāṇḍu}
\item[Partha] Epithet of Arjuna: son of Pritha/Kunti. \textit{Forms: Partha | Pārtha | Pṛthā}
\item[Vasudeva] Name or epithet of Krishna, son of Vasudeva. \textit{Forms: Vāsudeva | Vasudeva}
\end{description}
